## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?


### Proposed method
Freeze-and-Residual with Anchor Distillation (FRAD)

1) Freeze the base, add small residuals, and snapshot a teacher. Copy the current model M0 as a frozen teacher and keep it for reference. In the student, freeze all base weights and insert lightweight residual adaptation modules R (e.g., LoRA/adapters after feed‑forward blocks and a TaskRes-style residual on the task head). Zero‑initialize R so behavior initially matches M0. Add a simple gate g(x) that can bypass R at inference for inputs that look like prior domains (estimated by a domain discriminator or teacher–student agreement score).

2) Build a data‑free “anchor” distribution that approximates prior knowledge without storing old data. For language models, sample prompts from public corpora and from M0 itself (nucleus/top‑k) to form unlabeled anchors; for vision or other modalities, use broad unlabeled web data or synthesize pseudo‑inputs via feature inversion so their intermediate activations match stored teacher statistics. Instead of retaining raw historical data, maintain a tiny summary memory per layer: a set of activation centroids and low‑rank bases (from online PCA over teacher features on anchors). Optionally keep a few synthetic pseudo‑exemplars generated to match these centroids; discard any real prior examples.

3) Train with alternating preservation and acquisition steps, updating only R (and optionally layer norms). On new task batches Dnew, optimize the standard task loss Lnew (e.g., cross‑entropy or next‑token loss). On anchor batches A, preserve prior behavior via multi‑level distillation: minimize KL(pM0(y|x) || pM(y|x)) at the output and match intermediate features to the stored centroids/bases (e.g., L2 on projected activations). Add a small L2/orthogonality penalty that keeps R low‑norm and discourages changes along directions of high teacher sensitivity. Alternate A and Dnew mini‑batches (or mix with a tunable ratio ρ), using temperature‑scaled KL on anchors to stabilize training.

4) Route at inference and adapt online. Use g(x) to disable residuals on inputs likely from old domains (ensuring exact preservation) and enable R on new‑domain inputs. In streaming settings, refine R from an error buffer built from mispredicted queries, while sampling anchors in parallel to maintain stability. Follow a mixture schedule that ensures diversity across in‑distribution and OOD clusters when drawing anchors and updates, and track retention and adaptation with a small held‑out probe of anchors synthesized on the fly (no private data retained).

### Rationale
Freezing the base model stops destructive overwrites that cause catastrophic forgetting; confining changes to small residual modules learns new behaviors additively. Anchoring the student to a frozen teacher through KL and feature‑level distillation on unlabeled/synthetic inputs preserves prior functions without needing to store original training data. Alternating optimization over new‑task and anchor batches stabilizes learning under distribution shift, while a lightweight inference gate ensures backward compatibility by bypassing residuals on legacy inputs.

The summary memory (centroids/PCA) and pseudo‑exemplars capture sufficient statistics of prior representations, enabling data‑free preservation. In streaming deployments, drawing updates from error streams and balanced anchor sampling maintains performance under non‑stationarity while providing clear retention/adaptation metrics.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs (2310.20150)
   problem: LLMs need a way to remove the influence of specific, user-requested training data without retraining the entire model, while preserving task performance and making the forgotten information hard to extract.
   method:  Introduce lightweight unlearning layers (adapters) inserted after the feed-forward networks in transformer layers, and update only these small modules instead of the full LLM. Train them with a selective teacher–student objective: match the original model on retained data while deliberately deviating on data to forget by minimizing KL on retained examples and maximizing KL on forgotten ones. Add a task loss on retained data to maintain performance and a negated pretraining objective (e.g., masked LM loss for T5 with a 'Predict the masked word' prefix) on forgotten data to erase pretrained knowledge traces. Alternate optimization over retained and forgotten sets for stability, tuning only ~0.5% of parameters.

2. [LLM_NLP] On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams (2205.02014)
   problem: Deployed NLP models face boundary-agnostic, non-stationary OOD data streams and must be continually refined from their own prediction errors without catastrophic forgetting, while maintaining upstream/online knowledge and generalizing to unseen data.
   method:  Introduce the Continual Model Refinement (CMR) formulation that operates on error streams Et extracted from incoming queries Qt (mis-predictions by the current model). Provide a comprehensive evaluation protocol with five metrics—instant Error-Fixing Rate (EFR), Upstream/Online Knowledge Retention (UKR/OKR), Cumulative Success Rate (CSR), and Knowledge Generalization (KG)—and an overall criterion (OEC). Propose a general sampling algorithm to generate dynamic OOD query streams: at each episode, sample from (i) in-distribution V0 with a decaying proportion controlled by α, (ii) a major OOD cluster Vc_t chosen via a Markov chain with transition probability β, and (iii) remaining OOD clusters to ensure diversity controlled by γ; use multiple validation/testing streams for robust benchmarking.

3. [MULTIMODAL] Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models (2211.10277)
   problem: Efficiently adapting large vision-language models like CLIP to few-shot downstream tasks often either forgets valuable prior knowledge (prompt tuning builds a new classifier) or over-relies on pre-trained features (adapter-style tuning), limiting task-specific learning.
   method:  Task Residual Tuning (TaskRes) freezes the pre-trained text-based classifier (the CLIP text embeddings) and learns a prior-independent residual matrix x that is element-wise added and scaled to form the target classifier t' = t + αx. Only x is optimized while keeping the base classifier and image branch fixed, preserving prior knowledge and enabling flexible, task-specific learning without invoking the text encoder each step. Ablations show t + αx surpasses adapter-style t + αφω(t) on ImageNet few-shot (Table 2), and TaskRes achieves SOTA/SOTA-comparable results across 11 datasets and shots while being simple to implement (Sections 4.2, 5.3.1; Tables 2–3).

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment (2304.04514)
   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

6. [MULTIMODAL] End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning (2203.15350)
   problem: In Transformer-based captioning, vision–language interaction typically occurs only at cross-attention, leaving the decoder without global visual context early in processing, which weakens reasoning over the image.
   method:  PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.

7. [CV] S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging (2412.13156)
   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

8. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

9. [CV] Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (2107.11787)
   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

10. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

11. [RL] TempLe: Learning Template of Transitions for Sample Efficient Multi-task RL (2002.06659)
   problem: Multi-task RL often wastes samples or suffers negative transfer when directly reusing models/policies across tasks, especially when state/action spaces vary and inter-task mappings are unknown.
   method:  Introduce Transition Templates (TTs) that abstract a state-action’s dynamics by sorting its transition probability vector in descending order and pairing it with the reward, enabling grouping of state-action pairs with similar transition patterns across tasks of possibly different sizes. The Online Template Learning (O-TempLe) algorithm estimates rough dynamics after a small visit threshold ms, generates a TT (GEN-TT), assigns it to a TT group using a distance threshold τ, and then bi-directionally shares experience via TT-UPDATE and AUGMENT to ‘permute back’ and refine each pair’s estimates. It plugs into a model-based base learner (RMax) for policy updates when pairs become m-known, and compares TTs across different-sized tasks by padding zeros, yielding sample-efficient transfer without prior inter-task mappings and avoiding negative transfer.

12. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)
   problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
   method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

15. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

16. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

17. [SPEECH] DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR (2506.01916)
   problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
   method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

18. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: ASR target choices (characters vs subwords) have complementary error patterns; single-resolution training can overfit and underperform on rare words or syntax.
   method:  Use multiresolution multitask learning: a shared Transformer decoder predicts both character- and subword-level transcriptions, with separate output layers, trained via a weighted sum of losses (γ controls the balance; γ=0.5 performs best). This acts as regularization and reduces decoding steps for subwords while retaining fine-grained character supervision, yielding around 50% faster convergence and up to ~18% relative WER improvement over subword-only models on How2.

19. [ROBOTICS] Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning (1708.04225)
   problem: Many manipulation tasks depend on multiple objects whose relative positions matter, requiring perception that can jointly localize several task-relevant items and feed a compact state to the policy.
   method:  Stack multiple attention vectors in W to attend to several objects simultaneously over the shared meta-attention proposals and semantic features. Learn these attentions from a small set of kinesthetic demonstrations (initialized with example crops), then feed the attended boxes to a guided policy search controller. Demonstrated on a sweeping task (orange into dustpan) with varying object positions, achieving strong performance and robustness to distractors.

20. [ROBOTICS] Implicit Kinematic Policies: Unifying Joint and Cartesian Action Spaces in End-to-End Robot Learning (2203.01983)
   problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies are highly sensitive to the choice of action space (joints vs. Cartesian), and explicit policies that try to use multiple spaces face conflicting gradients and loss-balancing issues, making it hard for a single model to exploit the strengths of each space across diverse manipulation tasks.
   method:  Implicit Kinematic Policies (IKP) embed forward kinematics as a differentiable module inside an implicit behavioral cloning policy (energy-based model). Actions are sampled in joint space, transformed via FK to consistent Cartesian representations, and both joint and Cartesian actions (including 6-DoF poses for every robot link) are concatenated with observations and fed into an autoregressive EBM; sampling-based, derivative-free optimization selects low-energy actions dimension-by-dimension. This kinematically consistent, multi-action-space input lets the model discover and leverage low-frequency structure present in either representation without loss weighting or IK solvers, yielding performance on par with or better than the best single-space baselines (e.g., 85.9% sweeping, 97.5% flipping).

21. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

1. A study on data-free knowledge distillation for non-IID problems in federated continual learning (International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 0)
   The Federated Class-Continual Learning (FCCL) approach is a recently proposed method that has garnered widespread attention for its ability to enable multiple clients to collaboratively train a global model without exposing private data. This training paradigm protects user privacy without violating supervisory protocols and leverages distributed data to unleash its full potential. However, the data samples on each participating device in FCCL are typically non-independent and identically distributed (Non-IID), leading to severe statistical heterogeneity issues that pose significant challenges to FL. Existing FCCL methods face various limitations, such as requiring additional datasets or storing private data from previous tasks. To address these issues, we propose a novel method called GADG, which effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in FCCL and addresses Non-IID data challenges, all while preserving client data privacy. Specifically, our approach eliminates the need for complex data generators or data storage. Instead, it synthesizes pseudo-datasets for each client solely based on their local models. To further tackle the label distribution shift caused by Non-IID data, GADG introduces an adaptive mechanism that dynamically determines the label distribution and quantity of synthetic data. We conduct extensive experiments on multiple datasets, and the results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baseline approaches in terms of performance.

2. Dynamic Routing and Distillation-Aware Fusion for LoRA-Based Continual Learning (2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science and Blockchain (CCSB) 2025, cites: 0)
   Continual Learning (CL) for large pre-trained models faces the challenge of knowledge forgetting when adapting to new data. While parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques like LoRA show promise in mitigating catastrophic forgetting, existing methods either indiscriminately accumulate modules or fail to maintain knowledge consistency. We propose Dynamic Routing and Distillation-Aware Fusion for LoRA-based Continual Learning (DRDF-LoRA), a novel framework that introduces a dynamic LoRA selection mechanism to reuse historical knowledge modules in a task-boundary-free manner, and performs elastic LoRA fusion guided by behavioral distillation from earlier stages. We conduct experiments on challenging continual visual question answering (VQA-CL) benchmarks. The results demonstrate that DRDF-LoRA significantly outperforms baseline methods in terms of accuracy, knowledge retention, and parameter efficiency, showcasing its ability to achieve long-term knowledge preservation after fine-tuning large models on complex tasks.

3. Variational Data-Free Knowledge Distillation for Continual Learning (IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence 2023, cites: 34)
   Deep neural networks suffer from catastrophic forgetting when trained on sequential tasks in continual learning. Various methods rely on storing data of previous tasks to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, which is prohibited in real-world applications considering privacy and security issues. In this paper, we consider a realistic setting of continual learning, where training data of previous tasks are unavailable and memory resources are limited. We contribute a novel knowledge distillation-based method in an information-theoretic framework by maximizing mutual information between outputs of previously learned and current networks. Due to the intractability of computation of mutual information, we instead maximize its variational lower bound, where the covariance of variational distribution is modeled by a graph convolutional network. The inaccessibility of data of previous tasks is tackled by Taylor expansion, yielding a novel regularizer in network training loss for continual learning. The regularizer relies on compressed gradients of network parameters. It avoids storing previous task data and previously learned networks. Additionally, we employ self-supervised learning technique for learning effective features, which improves the performance of continual learning. We conduct extensive experiments including image classification and semantic segmentation, and the results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on continual learning benchmarks.

4. Diffusion-Driven Data Replay: A Novel Approach to Combat Forgetting in Federated Class Continual Learning (European Conference on Computer Vision 2024, cites: 29)
   Federated Class Continual Learning (FCCL) merges the challenges of distributed client learning with the need for seamless adaptation to new classes without forgetting old ones. The key challenge in FCCL is catastrophic forgetting, an issue that has been explored to some extent in Continual Learning (CL). However, due to privacy preservation requirements, some conventional methods, such as experience replay, are not directly applicable to FCCL. Existing FCCL methods mitigate forgetting by generating historical data through federated training of GANs or data-free knowledge distillation. However, these approaches often suffer from unstable training of generators or low-quality generated data, limiting their guidance for the model. To address this challenge, we propose a novel method of data replay based on diffusion models. Instead of training a diffusion model, we employ a pre-trained conditional diffusion model to reverse-engineer each class, searching the corresponding input conditions for each class within the model's input space, significantly reducing computational resources and time consumption while ensuring effective generation. Furthermore, we enhance the classifier's domain generalization ability on generated and real data through contrastive learning, indirectly improving the representational capability of generated data for real data. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/jinglin-liang/DDDR.

5. LoRA Subtraction for Drift-Resistant Space in Exemplar-Free Continual Learning (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 12)
   In continual learning (CL), catastrophic forgetting often arises due to feature drift. This challenge is particularly prominent in the exemplar-free continual learning (EFCL) setting, where samples from previous tasks cannot be retained, making it difficult to preserve prior knowledge. To address this issue, some EFCL methods aim to identify feature spaces that minimize the impact on previous tasks while accommodating new ones. However, they rely on static features or outdated statistics stored from old tasks, which prevents them from capturing the dynamic evolution of the feature space in CL, leading to performance degradation over time. In this paper, we introduce the Drift-Resistant Space (DRS), which effectively handles feature drifts without requiring explicit feature modeling or the storage of previous tasks. A novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach called Low-Rank Adaptation Subtraction (LoRA ) is proposed to develop the DRS. This method subtracts the LoRA weights of old tasks from the initial pre-trained weight before processing new task data to establish the DRS for model training. Therefore, LoRA– enhances stability, improves efficiency, and simplifies implementation. Furthermore, stabilizing feature drifts allows for better plasticity by learning with a triplet loss. Our method consistently achieves state-of-the-art results, especially for long task sequences, across multiple datasets. 1

6. Adaptive Federated Continual Learning for Heterogeneous Edge Environments: A Data-Free Distillation Approach (Global Communications Conference 2024, cites: 0)
   <no abstract>

7. Predictive Distillation Method of Anchor-Free Object Detection Model for Continual Learning (Applied Sciences 2022, cites: 3)
   Continual learning (CL) is becoming increasingly important, not only for storage space because of the ever-increasing amount of data being generated, but also for associated copyright problems. In this study, we propose ground truth’ (GT’), which is a combination of ground truth (GT) and a prediction of the teacher model that distills the prediction results of the previously trained model, called the teacher model, by applying the knowledge distillation (KD) technique to an anchor-free object detection model. Among all the objects predicted by the teacher model, an object for which the prediction score is higher than the threshold value is distilled into the current trained model, called the student model. To avoid interference with new class learning, the IoU is obtained between every object of the GT and the predicted objects. Through the continual learning scenario, even if the reuse of past data is limited, if new data are sufficient, the proposed model minimizes catastrophic forgetting problems and enables learning for newly added classes. The proposed model was learned in PascalVOC 2007 + 2012 and tested in PascalVOC2007, with better results of 9.6% p mAP and 13.7% p F1i shown in the scenario 19 + 1. The result in scenario 15 + 5 showed better results than the compared algorithm, with 1.6% p mAP and 0.9% p F1i. The scenario 10 + 10 also outperformed the other alternatives, with 0.9% p mAP and 0.6% p F1i.

8. TARGET: Federated Class-Continual Learning via Exemplar-Free Distillation (IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision 2023, cites: 109)
   This paper focuses on an under-explored yet important problem: Federated Class-Continual Learning (FCCL), where new classes are dynamically added in federated learning. Existing FCCL works suffer from various limitations, such as requiring additional datasets or storing the private data from previous tasks. In response, we first demonstrate that non-IID data exacerbates catastrophic forgetting issue in FL. Then we propose a novel method called TARGET (federatTed clAss-continual leaRninG via Exemplar-free disTillation), which alleviates catastrophic forgetting in FCCL while preserving client data privacy. Our proposed method leverages the previously trained global model to transfer knowledge of old tasks to the current task at the model level. Moreover, a generator is trained to produce synthetic data to simulate the global distribution of data on each client at the data level. Compared to previous FCCL methods, TARGET does not require any additional datasets or storing real data from previous tasks, which makes it ideal for data-sensitive scenarios.

9. DESIRE: Dynamic Knowledge Consolidation for Rehearsal-Free Continual Learning (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 6)
   Continual learning aims to equip models with the ability to retain previously learned knowledge like a human. Recent work incorporating Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning has revitalized the field by introducing lightweight extension modules. However, existing methods usually overlook the issue of information leakage caused by the fact that the experiment data have been used in pre-trained models. Once these duplicate data are removed in the pre-training phase, their performance can be severely affected. In this paper, we propose a new LoRA-based rehearsal-free method named DESIRE. Our method avoids imposing additional constraints during training to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, thereby maximizing the learning of new classes. To integrate knowledge from old and new tasks, we propose two efficient post-processing modules. On the one hand, we retain only two sets of LoRA parameters for merging and propose dynamic representation consolidation to calibrate the merged feature representation. On the other hand, we propose decision boundary refinement to address classifier bias when training solely on new class data. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple datasets and strikes an effective balance between stability and plasticity. Our code will be publicly available.

10. A novel data-free continual learning method with contrastive reversion (International Journal of Machine Learning and Cybernetics 2023, cites: 0)
   <no abstract>

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

Thought: The search returned relevant papers but none that exactly match FRAD's combination of: (1) frozen base + LoRA residuals, (2) frozen teacher snapshot for KL distillation, (3) data-free anchor generation via feature inversion/activation centroids/PCA, (4) inference gate to bypass residuals for old-domain inputs, and (5) online error-buffer streaming updates. Let me search more specifically for the inference routing/gating + adapter combination in continual learning.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?


### Proposed method
Freeze-and-Residual with Anchor Distillation (FRAD)

1) Freeze the base, add small residuals, and snapshot a teacher. Copy the current model M0 as a frozen teacher and keep it for reference. In the student, freeze all base weights and insert lightweight residual adaptation modules R (e.g., LoRA/adapters after feed‑forward blocks and a TaskRes-style residual on the task head). Zero‑initialize R so behavior initially matches M0. Add a simple gate g(x) that can bypass R at inference for inputs that look like prior domains (estimated by a domain discriminator or teacher–student agreement score).

2) Build a data‑free “anchor” distribution that approximates prior knowledge without storing old data. For language models, sample prompts from public corpora and from M0 itself (nucleus/top‑k) to form unlabeled anchors; for vision or other modalities, use broad unlabeled web data or synthesize pseudo‑inputs via feature inversion so their intermediate activations match stored teacher statistics. Instead of retaining raw historical data, maintain a tiny summary memory per layer: a set of activation centroids and low‑rank bases (from online PCA over teacher features on anchors). Optionally keep a few synthetic pseudo‑exemplars generated to match these centroids; discard any real prior examples.

3) Train with alternating preservation and acquisition steps, updating only R (and optionally layer norms). On new task batches Dnew, optimize the standard task loss Lnew (e.g., cross‑entropy or next‑token loss). On anchor batches A, preserve prior behavior via multi‑level distillation: minimize KL(pM0(y|x) || pM(y|x)) at the output and match intermediate features to the stored centroids/bases (e.g., L2 on projected activations). Add a small L2/orthogonality penalty that keeps R low‑norm and discourages changes along directions of high teacher sensitivity. Alternate A and Dnew mini‑batches (or mix with a tunable ratio ρ), using temperature‑scaled KL on anchors to stabilize training.

4) Route at inference and adapt online. Use g(x) to disable residuals on inputs likely from old domains (ensuring exact preservation) and enable R on new‑domain inputs. In streaming settings, refine R from an error buffer built from mispredicted queries, while sampling anchors in parallel to maintain stability. Follow a mixture schedule that ensures diversity across in‑distribution and OOD clusters when drawing anchors and updates, and track retention and adaptation with a small held‑out probe of anchors synthesized on the fly (no private data retained).

### Rationale
Freezing the base model stops destructive overwrites that cause catastrophic forgetting; confining changes to small residual modules learns new behaviors additively. Anchoring the student to a frozen teacher through KL and feature‑level distillation on unlabeled/synthetic inputs preserves prior functions without needing to store original training data. Alternating optimization over new‑task and anchor batches stabilizes learning under distribution shift, while a lightweight inference gate ensures backward compatibility by bypassing residuals on legacy inputs.

The summary memory (centroids/PCA) and pseudo‑exemplars capture sufficient statistics of prior representations, enabling data‑free preservation. In streaming deployments, drawing updates from error streams and balanced anchor sampling maintains performance under non‑stationarity while providing clear retention/adaptation metrics.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs (2310.20150)
   problem: LLMs need a way to remove the influence of specific, user-requested training data without retraining the entire model, while preserving task performance and making the forgotten information hard to extract.
   method:  Introduce lightweight unlearning layers (adapters) inserted after the feed-forward networks in transformer layers, and update only these small modules instead of the full LLM. Train them with a selective teacher–student objective: match the original model on retained data while deliberately deviating on data to forget by minimizing KL on retained examples and maximizing KL on forgotten ones. Add a task loss on retained data to maintain performance and a negated pretraining objective (e.g., masked LM loss for T5 with a 'Predict the masked word' prefix) on forgotten data to erase pretrained knowledge traces. Alternate optimization over retained and forgotten sets for stability, tuning only ~0.5% of parameters.

2. [LLM_NLP] On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams (2205.02014)
   problem: Deployed NLP models face boundary-agnostic, non-stationary OOD data streams and must be continually refined from their own prediction errors without catastrophic forgetting, while maintaining upstream/online knowledge and generalizing to unseen data.
   method:  Introduce the Continual Model Refinement (CMR) formulation that operates on error streams Et extracted from incoming queries Qt (mis-predictions by the current model). Provide a comprehensive evaluation protocol with five metrics—instant Error-Fixing Rate (EFR), Upstream/Online Knowledge Retention (UKR/OKR), Cumulative Success Rate (CSR), and Knowledge Generalization (KG)—and an overall criterion (OEC). Propose a general sampling algorithm to generate dynamic OOD query streams: at each episode, sample from (i) in-distribution V0 with a decaying proportion controlled by α, (ii) a major OOD cluster Vc_t chosen via a Markov chain with transition probability β, and (iii) remaining OOD clusters to ensure diversity controlled by γ; use multiple validation/testing streams for robust benchmarking.

3. [MULTIMODAL] Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models (2211.10277)
   problem: Efficiently adapting large vision-language models like CLIP to few-shot downstream tasks often either forgets valuable prior knowledge (prompt tuning builds a new classifier) or over-relies on pre-trained features (adapter-style tuning), limiting task-specific learning.
   method:  Task Residual Tuning (TaskRes) freezes the pre-trained text-based classifier (the CLIP text embeddings) and learns a prior-independent residual matrix x that is element-wise added and scaled to form the target classifier t' = t + αx. Only x is optimized while keeping the base classifier and image branch fixed, preserving prior knowledge and enabling flexible, task-specific learning without invoking the text encoder each step. Ablations show t + αx surpasses adapter-style t + αφω(t) on ImageNet few-shot (Table 2), and TaskRes achieves SOTA/SOTA-comparable results across 11 datasets and shots while being simple to implement (Sections 4.2, 5.3.1; Tables 2–3).

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment (2304.04514)
   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

6. [MULTIMODAL] End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning (2203.15350)
   problem: In Transformer-based captioning, vision–language interaction typically occurs only at cross-attention, leaving the decoder without global visual context early in processing, which weakens reasoning over the image.
   method:  PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.

7. [CV] S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging (2412.13156)
   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

8. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

9. [CV] Leveraging Auxiliary Tasks with Affinity Learning for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (2107.11787)
   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

10. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

11. [RL] TempLe: Learning Template of Transitions for Sample Efficient Multi-task RL (2002.06659)
   problem: Multi-task RL often wastes samples or suffers negative transfer when directly reusing models/policies across tasks, especially when state/action spaces vary and inter-task mappings are unknown.
   method:  Introduce Transition Templates (TTs) that abstract a state-action’s dynamics by sorting its transition probability vector in descending order and pairing it with the reward, enabling grouping of state-action pairs with similar transition patterns across tasks of possibly different sizes. The Online Template Learning (O-TempLe) algorithm estimates rough dynamics after a small visit threshold ms, generates a TT (GEN-TT), assigns it to a TT group using a distance threshold τ, and then bi-directionally shares experience via TT-UPDATE and AUGMENT to ‘permute back’ and refine each pair’s estimates. It plugs into a model-based base learner (RMax) for policy updates when pairs become m-known, and compares TTs across different-sized tasks by padding zeros, yielding sample-efficient transfer without prior inter-task mappings and avoiding negative transfer.

12. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)
   problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
   method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

15. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

16. [SPEECH] End-to-end Contrastive Language-Speech Pretraining Model For Long-form Spoken Question Answering (2511.09282)
   problem: Training the non-autoregressive ASR within the retriever is brittle—recognition errors degrade the learned token distributions and downstream retrieval quality; improving the decoder’s context modeling and stability is needed.
   method:  CLSR introduces a sampler for training: after a first-pass decode, it compares predicted tokens with ground truth, and selectively replaces embeddings of misrecognized tokens by their true text embeddings at a mixing ratio λ to form semantic features for a second decoding pass. This stabilizes token distributions used by the VQ adaptor and improves both WER and retrieval recall. Ablations show that removing the VQ quantizer severely harms retrieval, while enabling the sampler improves ASR and retrieval metrics (e.g., on Spoken-SQuAD, WER decreases and R@k increases).

17. [SPEECH] DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR (2506.01916)
   problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
   method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

18. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: ASR target choices (characters vs subwords) have complementary error patterns; single-resolution training can overfit and underperform on rare words or syntax.
   method:  Use multiresolution multitask learning: a shared Transformer decoder predicts both character- and subword-level transcriptions, with separate output layers, trained via a weighted sum of losses (γ controls the balance; γ=0.5 performs best). This acts as regularization and reduces decoding steps for subwords while retaining fine-grained character supervision, yielding around 50% faster convergence and up to ~18% relative WER improvement over subword-only models on How2.

19. [ROBOTICS] Deep Object-Centric Representations for Generalizable Robot Learning (1708.04225)
   problem: Many manipulation tasks depend on multiple objects whose relative positions matter, requiring perception that can jointly localize several task-relevant items and feed a compact state to the policy.
   method:  Stack multiple attention vectors in W to attend to several objects simultaneously over the shared meta-attention proposals and semantic features. Learn these attentions from a small set of kinesthetic demonstrations (initialized with example crops), then feed the attended boxes to a guided policy search controller. Demonstrated on a sweeping task (orange into dustpan) with varying object positions, achieving strong performance and robustness to distractors.

20. [ROBOTICS] Implicit Kinematic Policies: Unifying Joint and Cartesian Action Spaces in End-to-End Robot Learning (2203.01983)
   problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies are highly sensitive to the choice of action space (joints vs. Cartesian), and explicit policies that try to use multiple spaces face conflicting gradients and loss-balancing issues, making it hard for a single model to exploit the strengths of each space across diverse manipulation tasks.
   method:  Implicit Kinematic Policies (IKP) embed forward kinematics as a differentiable module inside an implicit behavioral cloning policy (energy-based model). Actions are sampled in joint space, transformed via FK to consistent Cartesian representations, and both joint and Cartesian actions (including 6-DoF poses for every robot link) are concatenated with observations and fed into an autoregressive EBM; sampling-based, derivative-free optimization selects low-energy actions dimension-by-dimension. This kinematically consistent, multi-action-space input lets the model discover and leverage low-frequency structure present in either representation without loss weighting or IK solvers, yielding performance on par with or better than the best single-space baselines (e.g., 85.9% sweeping, 97.5% flipping).

21. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).
