   Online Continual Learning (OCL) enables machine learning models to learn from a stream of non-stationary tasks, making it more aligned with real-world scenarios. However, OCL faces a significant challenge: catastrophic forgetting, wherein the model learned in previous tasks is substantially overwritten upon encountering new tasks, leading to a biased forgetting of prior knowledge. Among various OCL strategies, replay-based methods have proven particularly effective in mitigating catastrophic forgetting by maintaining a small buffer of past samples and retraining them alongside new data. However, due to strict memory constraints, these replay buffers often fail to adequately represent the true data distribution of previous tasks. This leads to distributional shifts in the feature space, amplifying forgetting and degrading model performance. To address the problem, in this paper, we propose a novel replay strategy, termed Dual-Margin Contrastive Replay (DMCR), to anchor the distribution of old tasks and reduce the negative transfer effects. First, we propose to select memory for more representative samples guided by constructed centroids in a data stream. Then, to keep the model from distribution chaos in biased replay, a two-level angular cross-task Contrastive Margin Loss (CML) is proposed, to encourage the intra-class and intra-task compactness, and increase the inter-class and inter-task discrepancy. Finally, to further suppress the distributional drift, we present an optional Centroid Distillation Loss (CDL) on the replay memory to anchor the knowledge in feature space for each previous old task. Extensive experimental results on five benchmark datasets validate that the proposed DMCR can effectively mitigate the catastrophic forgetting and achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in OCL.

2. Overcoming Domain Drift in Online Continual Learning (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 2)
   Online Continual Learning (OCL) empowers machine learning models to acquire new knowledge online across a sequence of tasks. However, OCL faces a significant challenge: catastrophic forgetting, wherein the model learned in previous tasks is substantially overwritten upon encountering new tasks, leading to a biased forgetting of prior knowledge. Moreover, the continual doman drift in sequential learning tasks may entail the gradual displacement of the decision boundaries in the learned feature space, rendering the learned knowledge susceptible to forgetting. To address the above problem, in this paper, we propose a novel rehearsal strategy, termed Drift-Reducing Rehearsal (DRR), to anchor the domain of old tasks and reduce the negative transfer effects. First, we propose to select memory for more representative samples guided by constructed centroids in a data stream. Then, to keep the model from domain chaos in drifting, a two-level angular cross-task Contrastive Margin Loss (CML) is proposed, to encourage the intra-class and intra-task compactness, and increase the inter-class and inter-task discrepancy. Finally, to further suppress the continual domain drift, we present an optional Centorid Distillation Loss (CDL) on the rehearsal memory to anchor the knowledge in feature space for each previous old task. Extensive experimental results on four benchmark datasets validate that the proposed DRR can effectively mitigate the continual domain drift and achieve the state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in OCL.

1. Escaping Stability-Plasticity Dilemma in Online Continual Learning for Motion Forecasting via Synergetic Memory Rehearsal (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Deep neural networks (DNN) have achieved remarkable success in motion forecasting. However, most DNN-based methods suffer from catastrophic forgetting and fail to maintain their performance in previously learned scenarios after adapting to new data. Recent continual learning (CL) studies aim to mitigate this phenomenon by enhancing memory stability of DNN, i.e., the ability to retain learned knowledge. Yet, excessive emphasis on the memory stability often impairs learning plasticity, i.e., the capacity of DNN to acquire new information effectively. To address such stability-plasticity dilemma, this study proposes a novel CL method, synergetic memory rehearsal (SyReM), for DNN-based motion forecasting. SyReM maintains a compact memory buffer to represent learned knowledge. To ensure memory stability, it employs an inequality constraint that limits increments in the average loss over the memory buffer. Synergistically, a selective memory rehearsal mechanism is designed to enhance learning plasticity by selecting samples from the memory buffer that are most similar to recently observed data. This selection is based on an online-measured cosine similarity of loss gradients, ensuring targeted memory rehearsal. Since replayed samples originate from learned scenarios, this memory rehearsal mechanism avoids compromising memory stability. We validate SyReM under an online CL paradigm where training samples from diverse scenarios arrive as a one-pass stream. Experiments on 11 naturalistic driving datasets from INTERACTION demonstrate that, compared to non-CL and CL baselines, SyReM significantly mitigates catastrophic forgetting in past scenarios while improving forecasting accuracy in new ones. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/BIT-Jack/SyReM.

2. Astrocyte-gated multi-timescale plasticity for online continual learning in deep spiking neural networks (Frontiers in Neuroscience 2026, cites: 0)
   Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) offer a paradigm of energy-efficient, event-driven computation that is well-suited for processing asynchronous sensory streams. However, training deep SNNs robustly in an online and continual manner remains a formidable challenge. Standard Backpropagation-through-Time (BPTT) suffers from a prohibitive memory bottleneck due to the storage of temporal histories, while local plasticity rules often fail to balance the trade-off between rapid acquisition of new information and the retention of old knowledge (the stability-plasticity dilemma). Motivated by the tripartite synapse in biological systems, where astrocytes regulate synaptic efficacy over slow timescales, we propose Astrocyte-Gated Multi-Timescale Plasticity (AGMP). AGMP is a scalable, online learning framework that augments eligibility traces with a broadcast teaching signal and a novel astrocyte-mediated gating mechanism. This slow astrocytic variable integrates neuronal activity to dynamically modulate plasticity, suppressing updates in stable regimes while enabling adaptation during distribution shifts. We evaluate AGMP on a comprehensive suite of neuromorphic benchmarks, including N-Caltech101, DVS128 Gesture, and Spiking Heidelberg Digits (SHD). Experimental results demonstrate that AGMP achieves accuracy competitive with offline BPTT while maintaining constant O(1) temporal memory complexity. Furthermore, in rigorous Class-Incremental Continual Learning scenarios (e.g., Split CIFAR-100), AGMP significantly mitigates catastrophic forgetting without requiring replay buffers, outperforming state-of-the-art online learning rules. This work provides a biologically grounded, hardware-friendly path toward autonomous learning agents capable of lifelong adaptation.

3. Latency-Aware Online Continual Learning for Non-Stationary Data Streams (IEEE Conference on Computer Communications 2025, cites: 3)
   <no abstract>

4. Holistic Continual Learning under Concept Drift with Adaptive Memory Realignment (Trans. Mach. Learn. Res. 2025, cites: 2)
   Traditional continual learning methods prioritize knowledge retention and focus primarily on mitigating catastrophic forgetting, implicitly assuming that the data distribution of previously learned tasks remains static. This overlooks the dynamic nature of real-world data streams, where concept drift permanently alters previously seen data and demands both stability and rapid adaptation. We introduce a holistic framework for continual learning under concept drift that simulates realistic scenarios by evolving task distributions. As a baseline, we consider Full Relearning (FR), in which the model is retrained from scratch on newly labeled samples from the drifted distribution. While effective, this approach incurs substantial annotation and computational overhead. To address these limitations, we propose Adaptive Memory Realignment (AMR), a lightweight alternative that equips rehearsal-based learners with a drift-aware adaptation mechanism. AMR selectively removes outdated samples of drifted classes from the replay buffer and repopulates it with a small number of up-to-date instances, effectively realigning memory with the new distribution. This targeted resampling matches the performance of FR while reducing the need for labeled data and computation by orders of magnitude. To enable reproducible evaluation, we introduce four concept drift variants of standard vision benchmarks, where previously seen classes reappear with shifted representations. Comprehensive experiments on these datasets using several rehearsal-based baselines show that AMR consistently counters concept drift, maintaining high accuracy with minimal overhead. These results position AMR as a scalable solution that reconciles stability and plasticity in non-stationary continual learning environments. Full implementation of our framework and benchmark datasets is available at: github.com/AlifAshrafee/CL-Under-Concept-Drift.

5. QCL-IDS: Quantum Continual Learning for Intrusion Detection with Fidelity-Anchored Stability and Generative Replay (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Continual intrusion detection must absorb newly emerging attack stages while retaining legacy detection capability under strict operational constraints, including bounded compute and qubit budgets and privacy rules that preclude long-term storage of raw telemetry. We propose QCL-IDS, a quantum-centric continual-learning framework that co-designs stability and privacy-governed rehearsal for NISQ-era pipelines. Its core component, Q-FISH (Quantum Fisher Anchors), enforces retention using a compact anchor coreset through (i) sensitivity-weighted parameter constraints and (ii) a fidelity-based functional anchoring term that directly limits decision drift on representative historical traffic. To regain plasticity without retaining sensitive flows, QCL-IDS further introduces privacy-preserved quantum generative replay (QGR) via frozen, task-conditioned generator snapshots that synthesize bounded rehearsal samples. Across a three-stage attack stream on UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS2017, QCL-IDS consistently attains the best retention-adaptation trade-off: the gradient-anchor configuration achieves mean Attack-F1 = 0.941 with forgetting = 0.005 on UNSW-NB15 and mean Attack-F1 = 0.944 with forgetting = 0.004 on CICIDS2017, versus 0.800/0.138 and 0.803/0.128 for sequential fine-tuning, respectively.

6. Rethinking Memory in Continual Learning: Beyond a Monolithic Store of the Past (Trans. Mach. Learn. Res. 2025, cites: 0)
   <no abstract>

7. Preventing Latent Rehearsal Decay in Online Continual SSL with SOLAR ( 2026, cites: 0)
   This paper explores Online Continual Self-Supervised Learning (OCSSL), a scenario in which models learn from continuous streams of unlabeled, non-stationary data, where methods typically employ replay and fast convergence is a central desideratum. We find that OCSSL requires particular attention to the stability-plasticity trade-off: stable methods (e.g. replay with Reservoir sampling) are able to converge faster compared to plastic ones (e.g. FIFO buffer), but incur in performance drops under certain conditions. We explain this collapse phenomenon with the Latent Rehearsal Decay hypothesis, which attributes it to latent space degradation under excessive stability of replay. We introduce two metrics (Overlap and Deviation) that diagnose latent degradation and correlate with accuracy declines. Building on these insights, we propose SOLAR, which leverages efficient online proxies of Deviation to guide buffer management and incorporates an explicit Overlap loss, allowing SOLAR to adaptively managing plasticity. Experiments demonstrate that SOLAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on OCSSL vision benchmarks, with both high convergence speed and final performance.

8. Continual Contrastive Learning for Non-Stationary Data Streams in Autonomous Systems (Journal of Multidisciplinary Knowledge 2025, cites: 0)
   Autonomous systems operate in environments where data distributions shift over time, making stability and adaptability essential. Conventional continual learning approaches struggle to retain past knowledge while acquiring new representations from non-stationary streams. This paper introduces StreamCLR, a continual contrastive learning framework that updates representations incrementally through selective memory replay and distribution-aware sampling. StreamCLR employs a dual-buffer strategy: a short-term buffer that captures recent transitions and a long-term buffer curated using diversity and uncertainty criteria. A contrastive loss with adaptive temperature scaling helps preserve inter-class separability even as distributions drift. To evaluate robustness, we test on three benchmarks simulating autonomous driving and robotic navigation, where lighting, terrain, and object distributions shift gradually or abruptly. StreamCLR reduces forgetting by 25–38% compared to state-of-the-art baselines while improving stability under sudden domain shifts. A real-world case study on a mobile robot demonstrates reduced navigation error and faster adaptation when encountering unfamiliar obstacles. The paper discusses open challenges including computational overhead, bias introduced by buffer sampling, and safety implications of model drift in mission-critical deployments.

9. Improving Performance in Continual Learning Tasks using Bio-Inspired Architectures (CoLLAs 2023, cites: 4)
   The ability to learn continuously from an incoming data stream without catastrophic forgetting is critical to designing intelligent systems. Many approaches to continual learning rely on stochastic gradient descent and its variants that employ global error updates, and hence need to adopt strategies such as memory buffers or replay to circumvent its stability, greed, and short-term memory limitations. To address this limitation, we have developed a biologically inspired lightweight neural network architecture that incorporates synaptic plasticity mechanisms and neuromodulation and hence learns through local error signals to enable online continual learning without stochastic gradient descent. Our approach leads to superior online continual learning performance on Split-MNIST, Split-CIFAR-10, and Split-CIFAR-100 datasets compared to other memory-constrained learning approaches and matches that of the state-of-the-art memory-intensive replay-based approaches. We further demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by integrating key design concepts into other backpropagation-based continual learning algorithms, significantly improving their accuracy. Our results provide compelling evidence for the importance of incorporating biological principles into machine learning models and offer insights into how we can leverage them to design more efficient and robust systems for online continual learning.

10. Online Class-Incremental Continual Learning with Adversarial Shapley Value (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2020, cites: 262)
   As image-based deep learning becomes pervasive on every device, from cell phones to smart watches, there is a growing need to develop methods that continually learn from data while minimizing memory footprint and power consumption. While memory replay techniques have shown exceptional promise for this task of continual learning, the best method for selecting which buffered images to replay is still an open question. In this paper, we specifically focus on the online class-incremental setting where a model needs to learn new classes continually from an online data stream. To this end, we contribute a novel Adversarial Shapley value scoring method that scores memory data samples according to their ability to preserve latent decision boundaries for previously observed classes (to maintain learning stability and avoid forgetting) while interfering with latent decision boundaries of current classes being learned (to encourage plasticity and optimal learning of new class boundaries). Overall, we observe that our proposed ASER method provides competitive or improved performance compared to state-of-the-art replay-based continual learning methods on a variety of datasets.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?


### Proposed method
Freeze-and-Residual with Anchor Distillation (FRAD)

1) Freeze the base, add small residuals, and snapshot a teacher. Copy the current model M0 as a frozen teacher and keep it for reference. In the student, freeze all base weights and insert lightweight residual adaptation modules R (e.g., LoRA/adapters after feed‑forward blocks and a TaskRes-style residual on the task head). Zero‑initialize R so behavior initially matches M0. Add a simple gate g(x) that can bypass R at inference for inputs that look like prior domains (estimated by a domain discriminator or teacher–student agreement score).

2) Build a data‑free “anchor” distribution that approximates prior knowledge without storing old data. For language models, sample prompts from public corpora and from M0 itself (nucleus/top‑k) to form unlabeled anchors; for vision or other modalities, use broad unlabeled web data or synthesize pseudo‑inputs via feature inversion so their intermediate activations match stored teacher statistics. Instead of retaining raw historical data, maintain a tiny summary memory per layer: a set of activation centroids and low‑rank bases (from online PCA over teacher features on anchors). Optionally keep a few synthetic pseudo‑exemplars generated to match these centroids; discard any real prior examples.

3) Train with alternating preservation and acquisition steps, updating only R (and optionally layer norms). On new task batches Dnew, optimize the standard task loss Lnew (e.g., cross‑entropy or next‑token loss). On anchor batches A, preserve prior behavior via multi‑level distillation: minimize KL(pM0(y|x) || pM(y|x)) at the output and match intermediate features to the stored centroids/bases (e.g., L2 on projected activations). Add a small L2/orthogonality penalty that keeps R low‑norm and discourages changes along directions of high teacher sensitivity. Alternate A and Dnew mini‑batches (or mix with a tunable ratio ρ), using temperature‑scaled KL on anchors to stabilize training.

4) Route at inference and adapt online. Use g(x) to disable residuals on inputs likely from old domains (ensuring exact preservation) and enable R on new‑domain inputs. In streaming settings, refine R from an error buffer built from mispredicted queries, while sampling anchors in parallel to maintain stability. Follow a mixture schedule that ensures diversity across in‑distribution and OOD clusters when drawing anchors and updates, and track retention and adaptation with a small held‑out probe of anchors synthesized on the fly (no private data retained).

### Rationale
Freezing the base model stops destructive overwrites that cause catastrophic forgetting; confining changes to small residual modules learns new behaviors additively. Anchoring the student to a frozen teacher through KL and feature‑level distillation on unlabeled/synthetic inputs preserves prior functions without needing to store original training data. Alternating optimization over new‑task and anchor batches stabilizes learning under distribution shift, while a lightweight inference gate ensures backward compatibility by bypassing residuals on legacy inputs.

The summary memory (centroids/PCA) and pseudo‑exemplars capture sufficient statistics of prior representations, enabling data‑free preservation. In streaming deployments, drawing updates from error streams and balanced anchor sampling maintains performance under non‑stationarity while providing clear retention/adaptation metrics.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs (2310.20150)
   problem: LLMs need a way to remove the influence of specific, user-requested training data without retraining the entire model, while preserving task performance and making the forgotten information hard to extract.
   method:  Introduce lightweight unlearning layers (adapters) inserted after the feed-forward networks in transformer layers, and update only these small modules instead of the full LLM. Train them with a selective teacher–student objective: match the original model on retained data while deliberately deviating on data to forget by minimizing KL on retained examples and maximizing KL on forgotten ones. Add a task loss on retained data to maintain performance and a negated pretraining objective (e.g., masked LM loss for T5 with a 'Predict the masked word' prefix) on forgotten data to erase pretrained knowledge traces. Alternate optimization over retained and forgotten sets for stability, tuning only ~0.5% of parameters.

2. [LLM_NLP] On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams (2205.02014)
   problem: Deployed NLP models face boundary-agnostic, non-stationary OOD data streams and must be continually refined from their own prediction errors without catastrophic forgetting, while maintaining upstream/online knowledge and generalizing to unseen data.
   method:  Introduce the Continual Model Refinement (CMR) formulation that operates on error streams Et extracted from incoming queries Qt (mis-predictions by the current model). Provide a comprehensive evaluation protocol with five metrics—instant Error-Fixing Rate (EFR), Upstream/Online Knowledge Retention (UKR/OKR), Cumulative Success Rate (CSR), and Knowledge Generalization (KG)—and an overall criterion (OEC). Propose a general sampling algorithm to generate dynamic OOD query streams: at each episode, sample from (i) in-distribution V0 with a decaying proportion controlled by α, (ii) a major OOD cluster Vc_t chosen via a Markov chain with transition probability β, and (iii) remaining OOD clusters to ensure diversity controlled by γ; use multiple validation/testing streams for robust benchmarking.

3. [MULTIMODAL] Task Residual for Tuning Vision-Language Models (2211.10277)
   problem: Efficiently adapting large vision-language models like CLIP to few-shot downstream tasks often either forgets valuable prior knowledge (prompt tuning builds a new classifier) or over-relies on pre-trained features (adapter-style tuning), limiting task-specific learning.
   method:  Task Residual Tuning (TaskRes) freezes the pre-trained text-based classifier (the CLIP text embeddings) and learns a prior-independent residual matrix x that is element-wise added and scaled to form the target classifier t' = t + αx. Only x is optimized while keeping the base classifier and image branch fixed, preserving prior knowledge and enabling flexible, task-specific learning without invoking the text encoder each step. Ablations show t + αx surpasses adapter-style t + αφω(t) on ImageNet few-shot (Table 2), and TaskRes achieves SOTA/SOTA-comparable results across 11 datasets and shots while being simple to implement (Sections 4.2, 5.3.1; Tables 2–3).

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

   method:  PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.

   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

8. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

   problem: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation trained with only image-level labels relies on sparse CAM seeds and off-the-shelf saliency maps that are used offline and not adapted to the target data, yielding low-quality pseudo labels and coarse boundaries.
   method:  AuxSegNet is a joint multi-task framework that treats semantic segmentation as the primary task and leverages multi-label image classification and saliency detection as auxiliary tasks, all sharing a backbone and trained end-to-end using only image-level labels and pre-trained saliency maps as initial pseudo labels. The classification branch produces CAMs; the saliency and segmentation branches use atrous conv decoders and are trained with losses on both initial and refined predictions against pseudo labels. Saliency pseudo labels are updated online by fusing the model’s refined saliency with pre-trained saliency through a dense CRF, while segmentation pseudo labels are generated from CAMs (and later their affinity-refined versions), enabling the auxiliary tasks to adapt to the target dataset and improve segmentation performance iteratively.

It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

   problem: Multi-task RL often wastes samples or suffers negative transfer when directly reusing models/policies across tasks, especially when state/action spaces vary and inter-task mappings are unknown.
   method:  Introduce Transition Templates (TTs) that abstract a state-action’s dynamics by sorting its transition probability vector in descending order and pairing it with the reward, enabling grouping of state-action pairs with similar transition patterns across tasks of possibly different sizes. The Online Template Learning (O-TempLe) algorithm estimates rough dynamics after a small visit threshold ms, generates a TT (GEN-TT), assigns it to a TT group using a distance threshold τ, and then bi-directionally shares experience via TT-UPDATE and AUGMENT to ‘permute back’ and refine each pair’s estimates. It plugs into a model-based base learner (RMax) for policy updates when pairs become m-known, and compares TTs across different-sized tasks by padding zeros, yielding sample-efficient transfer without prior inter-task mappings and avoiding negative transfer.

   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

1. A study on data-free knowledge distillation for non-IID problems in federated continual learning (International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 0)
   The Federated Class-Continual Learning (FCCL) approach is a recently proposed method that has garnered widespread attention for its ability to enable multiple clients to collaboratively train a global model without exposing private data. This training paradigm protects user privacy without violating supervisory protocols and leverages distributed data to unleash its full potential. However, the data samples on each participating device in FCCL are typically non-independent and identically distributed (Non-IID), leading to severe statistical heterogeneity issues that pose significant challenges to FL. Existing FCCL methods face various limitations, such as requiring additional datasets or storing private data from previous tasks. To address these issues, we propose a novel method called GADG, which effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in FCCL and addresses Non-IID data challenges, all while preserving client data privacy. Specifically, our approach eliminates the need for complex data generators or data storage. Instead, it synthesizes pseudo-datasets for each client solely based on their local models. To further tackle the label distribution shift caused by Non-IID data, GADG introduces an adaptive mechanism that dynamically determines the label distribution and quantity of synthetic data. We conduct extensive experiments on multiple datasets, and the results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baseline approaches in terms of performance.

2. Dynamic Routing and Distillation-Aware Fusion for LoRA-Based Continual Learning (2025 5th International Conference on Computer Science and Blockchain (CCSB) 2025, cites: 0)
   Continual Learning (CL) for large pre-trained models faces the challenge of knowledge forgetting when adapting to new data. While parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques like LoRA show promise in mitigating catastrophic forgetting, existing methods either indiscriminately accumulate modules or fail to maintain knowledge consistency. We propose Dynamic Routing and Distillation-Aware Fusion for LoRA-based Continual Learning (DRDF-LoRA), a novel framework that introduces a dynamic LoRA selection mechanism to reuse historical knowledge modules in a task-boundary-free manner, and performs elastic LoRA fusion guided by behavioral distillation from earlier stages. We conduct experiments on challenging continual visual question answering (VQA-CL) benchmarks. The results demonstrate that DRDF-LoRA significantly outperforms baseline methods in terms of accuracy, knowledge retention, and parameter efficiency, showcasing its ability to achieve long-term knowledge preservation after fine-tuning large models on complex tasks.

3. Variational Data-Free Knowledge Distillation for Continual Learning (IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence 2023, cites: 34)
   Deep neural networks suffer from catastrophic forgetting when trained on sequential tasks in continual learning. Various methods rely on storing data of previous tasks to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, which is prohibited in real-world applications considering privacy and security issues. In this paper, we consider a realistic setting of continual learning, where training data of previous tasks are unavailable and memory resources are limited. We contribute a novel knowledge distillation-based method in an information-theoretic framework by maximizing mutual information between outputs of previously learned and current networks. Due to the intractability of computation of mutual information, we instead maximize its variational lower bound, where the covariance of variational distribution is modeled by a graph convolutional network. The inaccessibility of data of previous tasks is tackled by Taylor expansion, yielding a novel regularizer in network training loss for continual learning. The regularizer relies on compressed gradients of network parameters. It avoids storing previous task data and previously learned networks. Additionally, we employ self-supervised learning technique for learning effective features, which improves the performance of continual learning. We conduct extensive experiments including image classification and semantic segmentation, and the results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on continual learning benchmarks.

4. Diffusion-Driven Data Replay: A Novel Approach to Combat Forgetting in Federated Class Continual Learning (European Conference on Computer Vision 2024, cites: 29)
   Federated Class Continual Learning (FCCL) merges the challenges of distributed client learning with the need for seamless adaptation to new classes without forgetting old ones. The key challenge in FCCL is catastrophic forgetting, an issue that has been explored to some extent in Continual Learning (CL). However, due to privacy preservation requirements, some conventional methods, such as experience replay, are not directly applicable to FCCL. Existing FCCL methods mitigate forgetting by generating historical data through federated training of GANs or data-free knowledge distillation. However, these approaches often suffer from unstable training of generators or low-quality generated data, limiting their guidance for the model. To address this challenge, we propose a novel method of data replay based on diffusion models. Instead of training a diffusion model, we employ a pre-trained conditional diffusion model to reverse-engineer each class, searching the corresponding input conditions for each class within the model's input space, significantly reducing computational resources and time consumption while ensuring effective generation. Furthermore, we enhance the classifier's domain generalization ability on generated and real data through contrastive learning, indirectly improving the representational capability of generated data for real data. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/jinglin-liang/DDDR.

5. LoRA Subtraction for Drift-Resistant Space in Exemplar-Free Continual Learning (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 12)
   In continual learning (CL), catastrophic forgetting often arises due to feature drift. This challenge is particularly prominent in the exemplar-free continual learning (EFCL) setting, where samples from previous tasks cannot be retained, making it difficult to preserve prior knowledge. To address this issue, some EFCL methods aim to identify feature spaces that minimize the impact on previous tasks while accommodating new ones. However, they rely on static features or outdated statistics stored from old tasks, which prevents them from capturing the dynamic evolution of the feature space in CL, leading to performance degradation over time. In this paper, we introduce the Drift-Resistant Space (DRS), which effectively handles feature drifts without requiring explicit feature modeling or the storage of previous tasks. A novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach called Low-Rank Adaptation Subtraction (LoRA ) is proposed to develop the DRS. This method subtracts the LoRA weights of old tasks from the initial pre-trained weight before processing new task data to establish the DRS for model training. Therefore, LoRA– enhances stability, improves efficiency, and simplifies implementation. Furthermore, stabilizing feature drifts allows for better plasticity by learning with a triplet loss. Our method consistently achieves state-of-the-art results, especially for long task sequences, across multiple datasets. 1

7. Predictive Distillation Method of Anchor-Free Object Detection Model for Continual Learning (Applied Sciences 2022, cites: 3)
   Continual learning (CL) is becoming increasingly important, not only for storage space because of the ever-increasing amount of data being generated, but also for associated copyright problems. In this study, we propose ground truth’ (GT’), which is a combination of ground truth (GT) and a prediction of the teacher model that distills the prediction results of the previously trained model, called the teacher model, by applying the knowledge distillation (KD) technique to an anchor-free object detection model. Among all the objects predicted by the teacher model, an object for which the prediction score is higher than the threshold value is distilled into the current trained model, called the student model. To avoid interference with new class learning, the IoU is obtained between every object of the GT and the predicted objects. Through the continual learning scenario, even if the reuse of past data is limited, if new data are sufficient, the proposed model minimizes catastrophic forgetting problems and enables learning for newly added classes. The proposed model was learned in PascalVOC 2007 + 2012 and tested in PascalVOC2007, with better results of 9.6% p mAP and 13.7% p F1i shown in the scenario 19 + 1. The result in scenario 15 + 5 showed better results than the compared algorithm, with 1.6% p mAP and 0.9% p F1i. The scenario 10 + 10 also outperformed the other alternatives, with 0.9% p mAP and 0.6% p F1i.

   This paper focuses on an under-explored yet important problem: Federated Class-Continual Learning (FCCL), where new classes are dynamically added in federated learning. Existing FCCL works suffer from various limitations, such as requiring additional datasets or storing the private data from previous tasks. In response, we first demonstrate that non-IID data exacerbates catastrophic forgetting issue in FL. Then we propose a novel method called TARGET (federatTed clAss-continual leaRninG via Exemplar-free disTillation), which alleviates catastrophic forgetting in FCCL while preserving client data privacy. Compared to previous FCCL methods, TARGET does not require any additional datasets or storing real data from previous tasks, which makes it ideal for data-sensitive scenarios.

   Continual learning aims to equip models with the ability to retain previously learned knowledge like a human. Recent work incorporating Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning has revitalized the field by introducing lightweight extension modules. Once these duplicate data are removed in the pre-training phase, their performance can be severely affected. In this paper, we propose a new LoRA-based rehearsal-free method named DESIRE. Our method avoids imposing additional constraints during training to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, thereby maximizing the learning of new classes. On the other hand, we propose decision boundary refinement to address classifier bias when training solely on new class data. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple datasets and strikes an effective balance between stability and plasticity. 1. CLARE: Continual Learning for Vision-Language-Action Models via Autonomous Adapter Routing and Expansion (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 1)
   To teach robots complex manipulation tasks, it is now a common practice to fine-tune a pre-trained vision-language-action model (VLA) on task-specific data. However, since this recipe updates existing representations, it is unsuitable for long-term operation in the real world, where robots must continually adapt to new tasks and environments while retaining the knowledge they have already acquired. Existing continual learning methods for robotics commonly require storing previous data (exemplars), struggle with long task sequences, or rely on task identifiers for deployment. To address these limitations, we propose CLARE, a general, parameter-efficient framework for exemplar-free continual learning with VLAs. CLARE introduces lightweight modular adapters into selected feedforward layers and autonomously expands the model only where necessary when learning a new task, guided by layer-wise feature similarity. During deployment, an autoencoder-based routing mechanism dynamically activates the most relevant adapters without requiring task labels. Through extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark, we show that CLARE achieves high performance on new tasks without catastrophic forgetting of earlier tasks, significantly outperforming even exemplar-based methods. Code and data are available at https://tum-lsy.github.io/clare.

1. Model Inversion with Layer-Specific Modeling and Alignment for Data-Free Continual Learning (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 1)
   Continual learning (CL) aims to incrementally train a model on a sequence of tasks while retaining performance on prior ones. However, storing and replaying data is often infeasible due to privacy or security constraints and impractical for arbitrary pre-trained models. Data-free CL seeks to update models without access to previous data. Beyond regularization, we employ model inversion to synthesize data from the trained model, enabling replay without storing samples. Yet, model inversion in predictive models faces two challenges: (1) generating inputs solely from compressed output labels causes drift between synthetic and real data, and replaying such data can erode prior knowledge; (2) inversion is computationally expensive since each step backpropagates through the full model. These issues are amplified in large pre-trained models such as CLIP. To improve efficiency, we propose Per-layer Model Inversion (PMI), inspired by faster convergence in single-layer optimization. PMI provides strong initialization for full-model inversion, substantially reducing iterations. To mitigate feature shift, we model class-wise features via Gaussian distributions and contrastive model, ensuring alignment between synthetic and real features. Combining PMI and feature modeling, our approach enables continual learning of new classes by generating pseudo-images from semantic-aware projected features, achieving strong effectiveness and compatibility across multiple CL settings.

You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 2112b9511767 [IR_REC] — "GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
- Method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

### Seed a9647aa8fe5e [IR_REC] — "Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
- Method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

### Seed 3917da5fd88a [LLM_NLP] — "Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
- Method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

### Seed 663d598bb0b9 [CV] — "KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
- Method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

### Seed 6bafdc8c3e0c [ROBOTICS] — "Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
- Method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

### Seed 9afe61a7b42b [ROBOTICS] — "Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
- Method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

### Seed 57b1e475a15e [IR_REC] — "Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models forget previously learned skills when fine-tuned on new
tasks; how can we update a model with new data without
catastrophic forgetting and without retaining all prior data?


### Proposed method
We propose Uncertainty-Guided Parameter-Isolated Adaptation (UPIA), a rehearsal-light continual learning recipe that updates a model with new data while preserving prior skills. The backbone is kept stable and augmented with a bank of lightweight, task-specific modules (adapters/LoRA for weights or prefix/visual prompts for inputs). A small routing head operates in the pre-logit latent space to choose which module(s) to engage per input. For each new task, we instantiate a fresh module and train it on the new data while freezing the backbone and previously learned modules.

Training a new module proceeds with two coupled objectives: task learning and functional preservation. Task learning optimizes the new head and its adapter/prompt using uncertainty-weighted gradients: examples and parameters with higher predictive or estimation uncertainty receive stronger updates, while confident regions are updated conservatively. Functional preservation uses self-distillation from a frozen reference model (backbone plus previously learned router/modules) evaluated on the same new-task inputs—no past data are stored. We match both logits and intermediate features so that representations used by old tasks remain stable where the new data overlaps them, and we regularize the backbone via a small, diagonal importance map accumulated online to prevent drift on parameters identified as critical for past tasks.

To route inputs and to decide when and how strongly to update, we attach a discriminative latent head with a set of trainable prototypes per task. At inference, cosine similarities to task prototypes drive a soft routing distribution over modules; a deterministic confidence score flags out-of-domain inputs, in which case the router backs off to the shared backbone or a mixture of experts. During training on a new task, the same prototypes provide a fast uncertainty signal to upweight difficult cases and to trigger expansion (adding a new module) rather than overwriting shared features when the model is uncertain.

Periodically, we compact and consolidate. Using the online importance map and cosine alignment between adapter-induced updates and backbone gradients, we selectively merge low-interference subspaces of mature adapters back into the backbone via low-rank fusion, then prune seldom-used modules. We retain only tiny, non-data artifacts per past task: the diagonal importance map and a handful of latent prototypes for routing/distillation—no raw examples. To further stabilize under distribution shift without rehearsal, we add a lightweight feature-alignment head (adversarial discriminator on shared features) so the new module learns domain-invariant representations compatible with the frozen teacher.

This end-to-end procedure yields bounded memory growth (small adapters/prompts + prototypes + importance stats), rehearsal-free preservation via on-the-fly self-distillation on current inputs, and adaptive optimization that concentrates change where the model is uncertain while shielding parameters deemed important for prior skills.

### Rationale
Catastrophic forgetting is driven by interference: updating parameters critical to old tasks to fit new data. UPIA reduces interference structurally by isolating most task-specific change to small adapters/prompts and by routing inputs to the right module, and functionally by distilling the frozen model’s behavior on current inputs so the shared representation and outputs do not drift in regions overlapping prior capabilities. Because distillation uses only the new data stream, no past examples are retained.

Uncertainty plays two roles: it gates update strength so that confident, well-established behavior is preserved, and it triggers expansion (new modules) only when needed, preventing destructive overwriting. The online importance map further protects parameters most responsible for prior tasks during any consolidation. Together, parameter isolation, rehearsal-free self-distillation, uncertainty-guided optimization, and selective consolidation provide a practical path to learn from new data without catastrophic forgetting and without storing all prior data.

Memory remains small and bounded: only lightweight modules, a diagonal importance vector, and a few latent prototypes per task are kept. Optional adversarial feature alignment improves cross-domain compatibility, further reducing reliance on replay while maintaining performance on past skills.

1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).
